semantic_description: Transfers a stitch from the left needle to the right needle without working it, inserted purlwise (right to left). The stitch maintains its original orientation and is not twisted. This is the default slip direction in most modern Western patterns when no direction is specified.

written_forms: slip the next stitch purlwise
slip 1 as if to purl
slip one purlwise to right needle
slip the next stitch without working it
slip as if to purl
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'sl1p' as slip 1 purlwise. The bare 'sl' or 'sl 1' without qualification is conventionally purlwise (this is the CYC default — unless knitwise is specified, slip purlwise). 'sl1' is extremely common. Distinguish from sl1k (knitwise).

Many patterns write just 'sl 1' without specifying direction. In most modern Western patterns, bare 'sl 1' defaults to purlwise UNLESS it precedes a decrease (sl1-k1-psso), where it is knitwise by convention.
In some British patterns, bare 'sl' defaults to knitwise. Always check the pattern's abbreviation key.

The abbreviation 'sp' appears in some patterns as shorthand for 'slip purlwise' but is non-standard and can collide with 'ssp' (slip slip purl) or be ambiguous. The CYC standard is 'sl1p'. Both are included here so users can find this op via search.

rs: Insert right needle into the next stitch from right to left (as if to purl)
Slide the stitch from left needle to right needle without working it

semantic_description: Transfers a stitch from the left needle to the right needle without working it, inserted knitwise (left to right). This twists the stitch — the leading leg changes sides. Used primarily as the first step of decrease operations like SSK, where the twist is corrected when the stitches are knit together.

written_forms: slip the next stitch knitwise
slip 1 as if to knit
slip one knitwise to right needle
slip as if to knit
insert needle knitwise and slip
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'sl1k'. Must be explicitly marked knitwise; bare 'sl' defaults to purlwise per CYC. Used in decrease setups (ssk, skp) and mosaic colorwork.

The abbreviation 'sk' appears in some patterns as shorthand for 'slip knitwise' but is non-standard and collides with 'SKP' (slip-knit-pass over, a decrease). The CYC standard is 'sl1k'. Both are included here for search discoverability.

rs: Insert right needle into the next stitch from left to right (as if to knit)
Slide the stitch from left needle to right needle without working it

semantic_description: Slips one stitch with the working yarn held at the back of the work (away from the knitter on RS). The yarn strand floats behind the slipped stitch. Used in colorwork, mosaic knitting, and textured stitch patterns. The float is invisible on the right side.

written_forms: slip 1 with yarn in back
with yarn at back of work, slip the next stitch
sl 1 keeping yarn at back
keeping yarn at back of work, slip stitch purlwise
sl1 with yarn held behind work
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'wyib'. In mosaic knitting, 'sl1 wyib' is an RS instruction (yarn behind = normal back position on RS). Combined slip+yarn-position abbreviations are very common in mosaic and slipped-stitch colorwork patterns.

'wyib' on RS means yarn is away from you. On WS, 'wyib' still means yarn is at the back relative to YOU (toward the RS of the fabric). This positional flip is the #1 source of errors in slip-stitch patterns.

rs: Ensure working yarn is at the back of work (away from you)
Slip the next stitch purlwise (unless pattern specifies knitwise)
Yarn remains at back — a float forms behind the slipped stitch

semantic_description: Slips one stitch with the working yarn held at the front of the work (toward the knitter on RS). The yarn strand floats in front of the slipped stitch, creating a visible horizontal bar. Used in textured patterns, heel flap construction, and decorative slipped-stitch fabrics.

written_forms: slip 1 with yarn in front
with yarn at front of work, slip the next stitch
sl 1 keeping yarn in front
keeping yarn at front of work, slip stitch purlwise
sl1 with yarn held in front
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'wyif'. In mosaic knitting, 'sl1 wyif' on an RS row brings yarn to front to create a float visible on the RS. Very common in slipped-stitch colorwork.

rs: Bring working yarn to front of work (toward you)
Yarn remains at front — a float forms in front of the slipped stitch

canonical_name: Slip 1 With Yarn In Back (2 Rows)
semantic_description: An elongated slip stitch that spans two rows in the chart grid. The stitch is slipped (not worked) on two consecutive rows, creating a longer float and a more pronounced elongated stitch effect. Common in mosaic and slip-stitch colorwork where a contrasting-color stitch needs to stretch over two rows of the background color.

alternate_names: two-row slip with yarn in back
written_forms: slip 1 with yarn in back on this row and the next
slip stitch over two rows keeping yarn in back
slip this stitch again on the following row, keeping yarn in back
slip the stitch stretching it over 2 rows with yarn in back
ambiguity_notes: A 2-row elongated slip is used in shadow knitting, brioche-adjacent, and some colorwork techniques. No standard CYC abbreviation — pattern-defined. The distinction from regular sl1wyib is that the stitch was slipped on the prior row and is slipped again without working.

rs: On the first row: slip 1 with yarn in back
On the following row: slip the same stitch again with yarn in back

canonical_name: Slip 1 With Yarn In Front (2 Rows)
semantic_description: An elongated slip stitch spanning two chart rows with yarn held in front. The stitch is slipped on two consecutive rows, creating a visible elongated float on the right side of the fabric.

alternate_names: two-row slip with yarn in front
written_forms: slip 1 with yarn in front on this row and the next
slip stitch over two rows keeping yarn in front
slip this stitch again on the following row, keeping yarn in front
slip the stitch stretching it over 2 rows with yarn in front
ambiguity_notes: Same as sl1_wyib_2row but with yarn in front. Pattern-defined.

rs: On the first row: slip 1 with yarn in front
On the following row: slip the same stitch again with yarn in front
